name: Snail Mail Windows/cross-port gameplay symbol crosswalk
notes: Use this as provenance for ownership and original C++ vocabulary, not as an address-based rename map.
The Windows function names remain the stable match harness identifiers.
source_object is from the v1.5 iPhone STABS-style object markers when available; v1.9-only symbols often lack an object file.
Android-only mappings use android_symbol instead of ios_symbol; their notes identify whether the body or only the exported name was compared.

entries.notes: Android preserves the same flag guard, hide-blend clear, and tail call into the owned cRToolTip reset. Every Windows caller discards EAX, and the void member remains exact at 9/9 instructions.
Android preserves the same hide-blend restore and 0x1000 flag clear. Every Windows caller discards EAX, and the void member remains exact at 5/5 instructions.

entries.android_symbol: cRBorder::Init(int, char*, int, float, float, tColour, int, float)
entries.notes: Android preserves the same shell-font widget construction and ends through the void RePosition member. All 83 Windows callers discard EAX; the void transcription remains 429/429 instructions at an honest 99.30%.
Android preserves the authored layout member and exits without a stable result. All 24 Windows callers discard EAX; removing the synthetic result leaves 177/177 instructions at an honest 84.18%.
Android preserves the same shortcut store and flag set as a void member. All five Windows callers discard EAX, and the corrected member remains exact at 6/6 instructions.
Android preserves the same anchor stores and tail-calls void RePosition. All 36 Windows callers discard EAX, and the corrected member remains exact at 9/9 instructions.

Android preserves the same hover-target clear and idle-padding selection. Windows callsites discard EAX, proving the copied padding bits are incidental; the void member remains exact at 4/4 instructions.

Android preserves the same hover-target raise and hot-padding selection. Windows callsites discard EAX, proving the copied padding bits are incidental; the void member remains exact at 4/4 instructions.

Android preserves the same text copy, cursor/length scan, marker append, blink/filter/capacity stores, RePosition call, and conditional cRInputOK initialization. Both Windows callers discard EAX, and the corrected void member remains exact at 46/46 instructions.

The exact Windows record and Android member share the 0x30-byte twinkle layout. Android cRTwinkleManager::Init(cRBorder*) stores the borrowed border pointer at +0x2c; Windows consumes the corresponding FrontendWidget flags and layout rectangle through that link.

Android preserves the same three texture slots, enable byte, wobble-direction byte, and three cleared padding controls. Both Windows callers discard EAX, and the void member remains exact at 14/14 instructions.

The exact Windows helper is a FrontendWidget method over the cRBorder layout; it tests either the padded layout bounds or the widget's authored texture-hit mask against player zero's saved cursor position.
The exact Windows member tears down the five widgets and clears the same 0x50-byte embedded owner used by Init, AI, and RegisterParcel.
All ports initialize the delivery-count, perfect-delivery, bonus, and five-widget phases on the same cRCompletion owner.
Promoting the Windows free fastcall transcription to the authored Completion member makes the 213-instruction function exact.
The exact Windows helper increments the delivered count, awards the parcel score, and advances the same Completion state machine.
The Windows thiscall uses the root-owned DirectXLoader and an owned Object. Its option tests consume the low byte of the authored 32-bit formal without changing the cross-port contract.
The exact Windows member initializes the 0x5e10-byte root-owned DirectX loader, including its fixed mesh cache and embedded duplicate-vertex workspace.
The exact Windows member searches or appends one entry in the DirectXLoader-owned fixed X-mesh cache and returns its slot index.

Windows and Android preserve the same path/duration parse-cursor ownership, keyframe-array construction, destination Object load, and RequestAnim handoff. Android assigns a clean missing-script flag value of 1; Windows instead ORs bit zero into the register reloaded from its keyframe-array pointer, exposing an older uninitialized-local bug.
The exact Windows member kills the three cRExit widgets, unhides the border batch, and restores the saved frontend state.

The exact void Windows member initializes the same state-driven Yes/No prompt layouts on the root-owned 0x1c-byte Exit. Android reaches a side-effect-only epilogue, and the sole Windows caller discards EAX.
The exact Windows state machine consumes the root Exit and is distinct from cRCompletion::AI at 0x404cf0.

Both mobile Open bodies contain the same authored galaxy-file parser, point rescaling, route/star-bank population, placeholder labels, and SubgameRuntime backlink. Windows splits that bootstrap into this no-argument void startup member and keeps the interactive card opener separately at 0x409c50.
The exact Windows member tears down the owned Star Map widget bank and clears active state.
The exact Windows member initializes the same Star Map widget and selected-route state on the embedded Galaxy owner.

Windows folds the mobile cRGalaxy::Render() route/icon pass into cRGalaxy::AI(); Android and iOS call those methods separately. Mobile cRGalaxy::AIControl() is only the result-state getter used by cRSubGame, while Windows returns that result directly from update_galaxy.

All ports open and reposition the selected route card, populate its title/detail widgets, and gate the replay control. The authored contract is void; decompilers expose only an incidental final widget-helper value.
The exact Windows helper computes the same widget bounds through the Galaxy member surface.

The Windows cRGame root method advances fade, audio, cheats, voices, cursor presentation, active BODs, both cRPlayer callbacks, sprites, and the per-frame contact registry before returning the root quit request.

The Windows caller ignores the result and both skip and common-tail exits preserve no meaningful return value, confirming the cross-port void method ABI. Android independently preserves the flagged cRBod -> borrowed cRAnimManager progress -> cRObjectAnim progress synchronization with port-specific offsets.

Both methods sample one controller slot into the same button-edge, axis, pointer, and authored-coordinate lanes before clearing the current button word. Windows owns two consecutive 0x70-byte cRGameInput records at GameRoot +0x44; each inherits the 0x38-byte cRBod-compatible base and installs the one-entry update_game_input table at 0x4972f0.

The Windows root constructs two 0x1f8-byte cRPlayer records and installs this exact function as their first callback; the method owns front-end dispatch plus the player camera, cursor, and overlay lanes. Both Windows and Android invoke the slot through a void cRBod callback, and Android tail-branches to the independently void tMatrix::Invert(tMatrix const&) helper after restoring its frame.

Both are one-store cRBackdrop members. Windows writes the normalized live track position to the owned float at +0x6c8; the stable Windows harness name is retained while shared source uses the authored zoom vocabulary.
All ports seed the owned backdrop distortion grid from one amplitude; Windows uses a fixed 8x8 BackdropDistortCell matrix while mobile sizes the equivalent grid from its active subdivision count.

The Android member has the same two parallel state transitions as Windows: invert blend, shift current world to previous world, and install the argument as current world. Both Windows callers discard the incidental return register, and the exact Windows source remains 14/14 when declared void.

The authored cRBackdrop render owner is stable across ports. Windows queues a fixed 7x7 warped quad mesh from the 8x8 distortion grid; mobile updates a platform VBO from the same phase/offset state before drawing it.

All ports consume staged backdrop changes and advance the distortion cells. Windows additionally dispatches split or single rendering from AI when the persistent +0x658 draw gate equals one; mobile calls cRBackdrop::Render() separately from the game render loop.
The sole Windows caller discards EAX. The independent iOS body invokes the actor's slot-zero AI callback and returns without establishing a result, proving the shared void contract.
The Windows receiver is the 0x1804-byte EnemyManager embedded at SubgameRuntime +0x1270fd4. Its one-store body is also identical-code-folded with an unrelated tracked-allocation stack initializer.
Both ports register a position, radius, kind, and cRBodPos-compatible owner in the fixed per-frame EnemyManager.

The mobile method preserves the separate floor and slide run scans. Windows proves the embedded SubLoc grid, SubRow attachment-body cleanup, and RootBodCatalog slice owners. Its sole caller discards EAX, but both native exits leave runtime_row_count, so analysis conservatively retains the integer result until the ABI is independently resolved.

Android carried this name first; the iOS SubGame.o grouping brackets the same track-normalization family. Windows stamps the owned SubLoc grid and has a void contract: its sole caller discards EAX, while the empty and populated exits leave incompatible incidental values.
Android preserves the full body: Yi row recovery, lane and row bounds, identical eight-lane indexing, IsEmpty call, and the same final tile exclusions 35/0/22/28.
Android preserves the owner and method name; both walk runtime cells and allocate directional cRFringe records from cRFringeManager.
Android preserves the full seed, selected-record, row-count, segment-selection, row-copy, and glyph-construction body, including the Segdif and Negative Segment Length diagnostics seen in Windows.
All ports ignore the receiver and render progress from containing-game state. Windows embeds the authored empty class at Player +0x3f0; cRWarning alignment accounts for the following three bytes.

The mobile dispatcher stores the level argument and calls SetFeatures, BuildColours, BuildLevel, PlaceParcels, SmoothTrack, WarnTrack, SlideSmoothTrack, CondenseTrack, and FringeEdgeTrack in the same nested rebuild order. Windows adds its platform-specific promotion and render-cache passes.

Android preserves the outer level-load, manager-reset, GenerateLevel, landscape, player, HUD, and music lifecycle. The Windows call to the shared one-byte stub with receiver SubgameRuntime +0x3bbb54 is the empty cRProgressBar::Init lifecycle edge also called by Android StartLevel.
Android body accepts the identical tile set 1/20/21/33/34/27; the Windows harness now follows the authored owner and method.
Android body accepts the identical authored ramp family 2 through 13.
Android body accepts the identical tile set 0/28/35/14/29.
Android body accepts the identical tile set 15/23/24/25/26/16/18/19; the authored name corrects the former Windows cache label that was opposite IsFloor.
The Windows cRFringe constructor table points directly to this exact callback. Android retains the class and method but its renderer-specific body is a no-op.

Android begins with Yi, handles attachment-row entry tiles, removes the cell BOD, and is called by both SubLoc::AI and SubGame::RemoveBods. Windows removes the embedded SubRow::attachment_body at row +0xb0, not the RowModel at +0x04.
The Windows SubLoc constructor table points directly to this helper; its tile dispatch, attachment-template backlink, and Yi/Remove siblings independently agree with the cross-port owner.
The exact Windows helper and Android body both emit an eight-particle collection burst from the borrowed SubHealth sprite; renderer-specific sprite IDs and field offsets differ.
The exact Windows and Android members test state +0x0c and clamp progress +0x00 to 0.94 on the same cRSubHover owner.

The mobile AI bodies advance the same progress/state lanes, warning envelope, borrowed cRSubGame/cRSubGoldy pointers, wobble outputs, jet teardown, runtime-row shutoff, and Hover call as the Windows 0x214-byte owner.
Both members kill the two sprite pointers in each of fifteen inline jet rows at cRSubHover +0x20.
Both initialize the same 15-by-2 inline jet sprite grid; the Windows fastcall-shaped decompiler surface is the no-argument cRSubHover member ABI.
All ports drive the owned jet sprite grid from the two snail jetpack hotspots and occasionally emit a detached trail puff.

Windows and Android clear the same cRSubHover state/wobble lanes, seed progress_step, and store the borrowed subgame and Goldy pointers at +0x200/+0x10. The Windows and Android integer parameter is unused; iOS v1.9 exposes the Goldy parent explicitly.
Both exact state transitions arm cRSubHover, clear its progress/wobble lanes, select the snail jetpack visual, and invoke JetInit.

Android retains the authored member and the same garbage-owned smoke-emitter role; the source-shaped Windows body allocates sprite 33 and seeds its color, size, velocity, position, and subgame-rate cadence from the SubGarbage receiver.

The iOS signature is v1.9-only. Android scans the fixed health array, initializes one slot, and branches to its epilogue without establishing a return value, supporting the side-effect-only method contract.
The exact Windows constructor table at 0x497320 points directly to this callback; both mobile ports retain the authored class and AI member.

The iOS signature is v1.9-only. Windows and Android both retain a literal no-op AddSpeedUp body. The same Windows address is also called with a cRSubHover receiver, Vector3 pointer, and float; Android's separate literal no-op cRSubHover::Hover(tVector&, float) proves that folded alias.
The exact Windows constructor table at 0x497314 points directly to this exact callback; both mobile ports retain the authored class and AI member.
Android scans and initializes the same singleton and reaches its epilogue without establishing a result, supporting the side-effect-only method contract.
The exact Windows constructor table at 0x497318 points directly to this callback; both mobile ports retain the authored class and AI member.

The sole direct Windows call overwrites EAX immediately, and the exact void reconstruction preserves all 143 instructions. Android leaves unrelated sprite or warning results in r0 on its success and exhaustion paths, confirming those values are incidental rather than a stable return contract.

The Android body independently proves the component-wise `(0, 0, -0.2) * subgame_rate` vector operation that makes the Windows allocator exact. The sole Windows caller discards EAX, while Android leaves the receiver on exhaustion and a blink-step value on success; however, changing Windows to void removes two real instructions, so the exact non-void spelling is retained without assigning a semantic result role.

v1.5 has AddRing(cRSubLoc*, int, cRSubGoldy*, float); v1.9 adds cRSubRow*. All five Windows callers discard EAX, while the iOS body returns from failure paths without establishing a result and ends by invoking cRSubRing::AI(), proving the shared void contract.
The Android name and body preserve the SubGarbage owner; the exact Windows helper clears state, removes the inherited BOD, kills the sprite, and unlinks the active chain.

The exact Windows constructor table at 0x497328 points directly to this slot-zero callback, which the active-BOD dispatcher invokes through a virtual void method. Both mobile ports retain the authored cRSubGarbage::AI member.

Android destroys the same three widget pointers at +0x00, +0x04, and +0x08 before releasing the mouse cursor. Its authored void contract agrees with every Windows caller discarding EAX; the Windows member remains exact at 22/22 instructions.

Android independently confirms the 0x0c-byte owner layout as Options +0x00, End Game +0x04, and Resume +0x08, then captures the mouse cursor. The side-effect-only Windows member remains exact at 88/88 instructions.

Android consumes the same three widget click bits and follows the Options, Resume, and End Game transitions without constructing a stable result. The honest void Windows member remains exact at 55/55 instructions.

All ports allocate the Click to Start border, store the borrowed Goldy parent, link the RenderableBod child, seed state, clear the parent's startup index, and apply the replay-launch hide gate. Android's RenderableBod base is 0x0c bytes smaller; every tail field shifts by exactly that amount.

The cross-port AI bodies share the prompt hide gate, state 2 input/replay handoff, parent startup-index write, pointer recenter and SFX 8, state 3 seed/teardown setup, and state 4 RenderableBod removal.
The Windows callback table at 0x497330 points directly to this exact helper. Its receiver is the 0x8c-byte RowModel embedded at SubRow +0x04, with velocity at model +0x80.
Both manager bodies walk the fixed parcel array and virtually dispatch each active cRParcel; the platform slot strides differ.
Both clear 50 inline parcel states and seed each borrowed containing-subgame backlink.
Both scan 50 inline records for the first inactive parcel and return null after the same exhaustion error.

Both dispatch the gameplay mode and level index into a local level path and then invoke the same owner's text-level parser. The authored member is void; every Windows caller discards the value incidentally forwarded from the parser in EAX.
Android and iOS v1.5 have AddParcel(tVector*, cRSubGoldy*); iOS v1.9 adds cRSubRow*. Android explicitly returns the allocated cRParcel pointer or null, matching Windows.
All three ports dispatch on the same cRSubGame receiver. The level definition and runtime rows are SubgameRuntime-owned; the normal digit/set candidate banks are global scratch.

All three ports use the same cRSubGame receiver and owned runtime rows. The filtered row-index bank retains the mobile symbol gParcelGroupSurvival0 and is global scratch rather than an embedded owner.

All ports select the runtime row from position.z, clear the angle output, dispatch nonlinear path kinds through a local matrix, and otherwise project sample position plus cell anchor plus lateral/right and vertical/up vector contributions.
The specific CameraAI spelling comes from cross-port evidence; iOS confirms cRCameraman and cRSubGame ownership in the same object group.

Both mask the receiver's low three lane bits, recover lane zero, subtract the runtime-cell slab base, divide by the platform cell stride, and divide by eight lanes. iOS uses a 0x44-byte cell; the exact Windows helper uses 0x54.
Both reset the manager allocation cursor before rebuilding track fringe objects.
Both return the next inline cRFringe record and advance the manager cursor; Windows proves a fixed 7000-entry, 0x38-stride pool.
Android uses the same state at +0x00 and 25 sprite slots at +0x18, then clears the owner after killing every slot. cRSubGoldy::AI() owns the teardown edge.
Android preserves the exact state, owner backlink, z-step/center, phase/step, and 25-slot offsets through the 0x7c-byte cRNuke layout; cRSubGoldy::Collision() calls Init.
Android and Windows advance the same z and angular fields and orbit all 25 sprite slots; Android cRSubGoldy::AI() calls the method through its embedded cRNuke.

The row-grid transpose and authored metadata copy agree. All three Windows callers load the SubTracks owner into ECX and pass two stack arguments before the callee returns with ret 8, proving the same void thiscall member despite its unused receiver. Windows reads the source through the root SMTracks owner, and its destination cRSubSegment is a 0x4220-byte inline payload rather than the compact later-port layout.

The parser, field order, and ImportSegment calls agree. The authored member is void; its Windows success and error paths leave unrelated cursor or diagnostic values in EAX, and all callers discard them.

The Windows raw input record is 0x48 bytes and feeds inline 0x4220-byte slots. Its inconsistent incidental return register plus discarded callsites prove a void Init contract; iOS later uses compact destination segments with separately allocated glyph storage.

Both parse segment text into the SMTracks owner with the same Name, Data, 3DModel, Velocity, Parcel, Path, ring, RingSpeed, NoFall, and JetPack metadata. Their common epilogues are value-agnostic and callers discard the result, proving the void contract.
Both enumerate authored level filenames through the same SMTracks owner and hand each name to the SubTracks text parser; neither returns a semantic value.

The 0x20-byte Tip and 0x14-byte TipData field order agrees across ports. Windows reads the hide-disable-button formal as a full 32-bit stack argument, while iOS v1.5 names it bool and v1.9 names it signed char, so the Windows declaration intentionally retains int32_t.
The exact Windows member drives the same Tip-owned widgets, authored TipData flags, dismiss progress, and frontend-owner restoration path.
Both initialize the manager's three inline 0x20-byte Tips. Their array begins at Windows +0x38 and iOS +0x2c because the port-specific BodBase prefixes differ.
Both walk the three inline Tips and tear down any active Tip-owned widgets.

The exact Windows member selects the first inactive inline Tip, initializes it from TipData, and returns that Tip. As with cRTip::Init, Windows retains the observed 32-bit hide-disable-button formal while later iOS changes the mobile spelling from bool to signed char.
Both walk the same three inline Tips and call AI only for active entries.
The exact Windows member seeds state and the borrowed containing-subgame pointer, then applies the same tutorial runtime flags.
The exact Windows thunk tears down tips through the root TipManager; both mobile ports retain the authored owner.
The exact Windows member samples the live Goldy position through its borrowed SubgameRuntime pointer.
Both clear global voice progress and the cooldown fields of the 16 inline VoiceSets. The Windows member is exact.
Both parse the same 16 named VoiceSets at a 0x18-byte stride, register their authored samples, read normalization and frequency settings, and return void.
Both update all 16 inline VoiceSets and advance the manager-wide voice timer. The Windows member is exact.

Both gate playback by the current voice and global frequency, optionally stop the active sample, and dispatch through the selected inline VoiceSet. The Windows member is exact; its recovered mode formal remains unsigned because that spelling preserves the observed Windows comparison.
Android preserves the same two zeroed font-wave phases and two fixed phase increments. The exact Windows routine is void.

entries.android_symbol: G0RenderFont(cRTexture*, float, float, float, float, float, float, float, float, float, float, float, float, float, float, tColour&, int, float)
entries.notes: Both build and submit one immediate textured UI quad from explicit corners, axis-aligned dimensions, or a rotation angle. All three Windows callsites discard EAX, and the 98.34% Windows reconstruction already has a void body.

Windows splits the cross-port G0SetBlend responsibility into object and immediate-quad Direct3D variants. This object variant is called only by render_object; the caller discards EAX, and recovering the void contract is codegen-neutral at 92.39%.

Windows splits the cross-port G0SetBlend responsibility into object and immediate-quad Direct3D variants. This immediate variant is called only by G0RenderFont; the caller discards EAX, and recovering the void contract is codegen-neutral at 85.50%.

Both reset the font text cursor and queue counters, advance the two wave phases, and wrap them at 2pi. Both Android callers and the sole Windows caller discard the incidental result, and the exact Windows routine remains void.
Android preserves the authored font remapper name and the same alphabet, digit, punctuation, icon-token, and fallback slot families.
Both accumulate per-glyph atlas widths through the remapper and append the scaled trailing-space adjustment.
Android preserves the atlas registrar, per-glyph delimiter scan, font count, scale parameters, and returned post-increment font index; texture container details differ by platform.

The mangled Android signature independently recovers cFontPrintBuffer ownership. Both bodies align text, map glyphs, apply wave offsets, and submit atlas quads; their queue drains discard the incidental terminating-byte result, proving a void source contract.

The mangled Android signature independently recovers cFontPrintBuffer ownership. Both forward the explicit quad coordinates, texture, UVs, color, layer, and blend fields; the only Windows caller discards EAX, and the exact forwarder remains byte-identical as void.
Both walk the cFontPrintBuffer queue backward, filter high-byte render flags, and dispatch text versus explicit-quad entries. The sole Windows caller discards EAX, and the exact routine remains void.

entries.android_symbol: FontPrint(char*, int, float, float, float, int, float, int, tColour, float, bool)
entries.notes: Android preserves the same queue gate, 0x84-byte cFontPrintBuffer stride, text-buffer cursor and cap, field layout, and queue-count increment.

entries.android_symbol: FontType(char*, int, float, float, float, float*, float*, float*, float*, float, bool, int, float, int, tColour, bool, bool)
entries.notes: Both split authored text into line runs, measure and optionally queue each run, apply alignment, and publish the block origin and bounds through four output floats.
Both materialize one 3D object per registered glyph from the font atlas and cache its width scale. Windows callers discard EAX and the recovered routine is void.

All ports initialize the same owned Goldy actor and its embedded cRSubHover child. Windows and Android also bind the animated cRSnail/cRWeapon BODs to their self-owned cRAnimManager and donor-slot banks consumed by cRGame::Render. The Windows caller discards the incidental register value; the recovered source contract is void.
v1.5 has GhostInit(int); v1.9 has GhostInit(int, int).
v1.9-only spelling preserves the shipped typo.
v1.9-only spelling preserves the shipped typo.

Windows and Android clear the same state, progress, progress-step, and completion fields. Android returns directly, proving that the exact Windows EAX zero is only a store source and the authored contract is void.

Android independently reads the same cRAnimManager offsets through +0x44 and preserves the state, flag, clamp, queue, borrowed cRBod target, cRBod slot, cRObject, and next-animation flow. Windows slots are 0x80-byte RenderableBod owners; record stride and Object::animation offset differ by port.

The Windows name is a stable historical harness identifier, not ownership evidence. Its sole caller passes Player +0x4344, and the Windows and Android bodies clear the same six-float Squidge layout; iOS exports the matching authored family. The real score buckets are at Player +0x310. cRSubGoldy::ScoreStatsInit() remains a distinct mobile symbol without a proven Windows mapping.
The Windows and Android bodies reset the Y phase and seed Y velocity on the same six-float owner; iOS exports the matching method, and Windows callsites pass Player +0x4344.
The Windows and Android bodies seed the Z phase and velocity lanes on the same six-float Squidge owner; iOS exports the matching method.

The Windows and Android bodies advance and damp the same independent Y/Z spring lanes; iOS exports the matching AI method, and Windows publishes the outputs through the Player presentation visual root.
The Windows and Android bodies reset the same embedded state machine and restore snail skin slot zero; iOS v1.9 exports the matching authored method.
Android confirms the authored Start owner and the same state, spin, fade, and render-bit transitions. Windows additionally emits its registered invincibility sound from this helper.
The Windows and Android bodies implement the same four-state fade/spin owner and skin transitions; the iOS v1.5 symbol supplies SubGame.o provenance.
v1.9-only signature.
All ports initialize the cRWarning phase state and border. Windows uses an exact 0x10-byte embedded owner; mobile variants add presentation fields before the border pointer.
Android cRSubGame::UnInit() calls this method to release the warning border, matching the Windows SubGame teardown edge.
Android cRDamageGuage::AI() calls Start on the embedded cRWarning, matching the Windows damage-gauge relationship.
All ports stop the warning state and hide its owned border.
The Windows member does not read its receiver and stops the shared warning sample; Android preserves ownership but ships a no-op body.
Android cRSubGoldy::AI() calls this method through the embedded warning owner; its state, phase, step, border-alpha, and sound-loop behavior match the Windows role despite the wider mobile layout.

The mobile signatures differ on whether the containing Goldy is explicit, but both initialize the exact 0x00..0x28 contact-gauge layout recovered on Windows. The shipped owner spelling is intentionally Guage.

Android AI reads the same 0x00..0x28 fields, calls Take on the same receiver, and is called from cRSubGoldy::AI. This corrects the former false mapping to the unrelated Windows jetpack-gauge composite.
All ports apply and clamp contact damage through the same 0x2c-byte cRDamageGuage owner.
Both exact bodies advance and clear the per-slug voice cooldown lanes; field offsets differ across the platform layouts.
Both gate on the slug and shared manager cooldowns, latch both active bytes, reset progress, and call the voice backend.
Both initialize the complete 0x0c-byte manager state: inactive, zero progress, and a 1/60 step.
Both advance positive progress and clear the active/progress lanes after the cooldown passes one.

Android and iOS v1.5 retain the side-effect-only no-argument member; iOS v1.9 adds cRSubGoldy*. Every Windows caller discards the result, and the void source form removes an artificial final zeroing instruction.
Windows and Android both gate on active state 1, transition through death-toss-pending state 2, and select right/left toss direction 1/2 from the slug's world x coordinate.

The exact Windows constructor table at 0x497324 points directly to this void callback; both mobile ports retain the authored class and AI member. Windows and Android agree on inactive 0, active 1, death-toss-pending 2, teardown-pending 3, and lateral-active 4 behavior.
Both walk 40 inline cRSalt records and clear the state at slot +0x80.
Windows and Android share the vector-only manager body; iOS v1.9 adds the owning Goldy argument.
The Windows cRSalt constructor table at 0x497340 points directly to this exact helper.

The exact Windows owner is one of two 0x60-byte Banner actors embedded at SubgameRuntime +0x359080. Android preserves the visibility mode, borrowed Player pointer, phase, and phase-step tail at the same offsets relative to its smaller platform BodBase.

All ports use the empty cRFireWork child embedded in cRSubGoldy/Player and emit the same randomized sprite burst. Windows passes its position by pointer and returns with ret 0x10; the mobile builds expose a by-value tVector signature.

Both walk the manager's 20 owned inline slots and clear each SubLazer state at +0x80. Windows' exact body remains byte-identical when declared void; the apparent end-pointer result is only the loop cursor left in EAX.
Windows and Android share the two-vector slot-level body; iOS v1.9 adds the owning Goldy argument.

Both clear the active state and unlink the inherited BOD node. The Windows helper remains exact at 43 instructions when declared void; Android's apparent result is the list-removal call value left in r0.

The Windows cRSubLazer constructor table at 0x49733c points directly to this helper. Native Windows control flow also preserves distinct primary and secondary attachment-hit debug markers before Kill.
All ports scan the manager's 20 owned SubLazer slots, dispatch the slot-level Shoot method, and play the positional fire cue.
The exact Windows helper clears the same six-field 0x18-byte value. Mobile returns directly after the stores, proving the void contract; Windows only uses EAX as its shared zero source.

Windows and Android share the 0x18-byte cRTime layout and the same add, rollover, and display-field updates. Android's direct return confirms that the final Windows EAX conversion result is incidental rather than an int return contract.
Both arm one ring effect and initialize ten inline cRSubRingStar children from the authored effect kind.
v1.9-only signature.
Both update one inline 0x20-byte child orbit around its borrowed cRSubRing parent before optional Shower dispatch.
The Windows constructor table at 0x49732c points directly here; both ports own the parent state machine and fixed child-star array.
The exact Windows constructor table at 0x497364 points directly to this callback; both mobile ports retain the authored class and AI member.

Windows and Android both ignore the explicit cRObject argument, read the retained output object at Vapour +0x24, store the half-width, allocate capacity + 1 transform slots, and tail-dispatch ReSet. Android establishes no semantic result, proving the void contract.
Android returns directly after clearing count, storing the borrowed float pointer, and clearing visibility; its unchanged receiver register proves the final Windows flags value is incidental.

Android clears or sets the same visibility bit, clamps the transform history, emits the quad strip through the retained cRObject, and returns without establishing a result. Windows' arithmetic residue in EAX is therefore incidental.
Both mobile ports retain this method on the same cRSnail owner; Windows uses the exact inline Snail at Player +0x2984.

The immediate and queued paths operate on cRSnail's root AnimManager and ten owned cRBod animation slots; each slot lends its cRObject and nested ObjectAnimation. The Windows harness name remains stable.
Despite the stable Windows name, both bodies transform the 19 local cRSnail hotspots through the two live source matrices into the world-space bank each frame.
All ports extract the authored hotpoint texture vertices into cRSnail's 19-entry local hotspot bank; the Android body independently confirms the member offsets and final CameraIntroTalk Y lift.
The exact Windows method advances the same state/progress pair and dispatches UnInit before killing the embedded cRSubGoldy.
The exact Windows member kills the borrowed border whenever the 0x10-byte cRTimesUp owner is active.
The exact Windows member allocates and initializes the Time's Up widget, then seeds state, progress, and progress step on the same owner.

The exact Windows and Android initializers clear selected_slot and active on the same 0x20-byte owner and store the borrowed cRSnail backlink at +0x10. iOS v1.9 exposes that parent explicitly as the Init argument.

The exact Windows and Android bodies follow the cRSnail backlink into its linked cRObject, dirty that object, select its material override from the three-slot bank, and advance the same active/progress/progress_step lanes.
The exact Windows and Android members arm the same skin slot and either apply it immediately or derive progress_step from the requested duration at 60 Hz.

The exact Windows and Android helpers clear state and write the same presentation/player backlinks at +0/+4. Windows and Android leave different incidental values in the return register, confirming the ignored source return is void; v1.9 iOS adds the cRSubGoldy* parameter.

v1.5 and Android use Init(); v1.9 adds cRSubGoldy*. Android writes the same three matrices, owner pointers, FOV, byte gate, and lift-envelope pair at identical offsets through +0xd4, proving the exact 0xd8-byte Windows owner.
Android cRSubGame::CameraAI() calls this method through the same cRCameraman owner and consumes the identical +0xc0..+0xd4 state window.

The Windows name is a stable historical harness identifier. Like Android cRSnail::AIGoldy, this per-frame presentation method runs snail-skin AI, matrix interpolation, hotspot construction, hover jets, animation dispatch, and the embedded cRCutScene::AI; it is not cRCutScene::Init.
Android confirms the same embedded owner, state at +0x0c, camera matrix at +0x10, progress pair at +0x50/+0x54, and intro/completion/death state families.

entries.ios_symbol: cRPath::BuildHump(float, float, int, bool, char*, char*)
cRPath::BuildDump(float, float, int, bool, char*, char*)

entries.notes: The byte-identical Windows helper is a void initializer on the Player-embedded FollowState. Its sole caller ignores EAX; the final Path-shaped register value is incidental, not a return contract.

Both builds store the interpolated matrix up basis as one owned FollowState vector. Both also retain the unreachable voice-4 guard at sample_index == segment_count * 2 even though traversal terminates at segment_count.
This Windows helper advances the Golb follow state; the similarly named no-argument `cRPath::CalcLengthZ()` maps to `finalize_path_template`.

entries.ios_symbol: cRPath::BuildSuperTramp(float, float, int, bool, char*, char*, char*)
cRPath::BuildP(int, float, int, float, float, int, char*, char*)

entries.notes: iOS also exposes `cRPath::BuildInvert1(...)`; this row preserves the known Windows family without asserting which original split survived the port.

Both linearly search the authored Path name table and return its index or -1. Windows stores the otherwise-empty PathManager at SubgameRuntime +0xff2910; the unused receiver makes its machine ABI indistinguishable from the prior stdcall presentation.
The direct Windows world-init call constructs public authored slot 42 (HALFPIPE); Android independently supplies the matching BuildHalfPipe name.
The iOS symbol confirms the nonlinear kind-42 transform family; Windows keeps the neutral helper name until HalfPipe/HalfPole splits are fully resolved.
Both Windows callsites discard EAX; the byte-identical VC6 candidate remains exact as a void method and naturally leaves the final z bits in EAX, so no synthetic scalar return is owned.
No-argument iOS method aligns with the template finalization pass that recomputes derived length scalars.

The iOS name describes the same swept-search responsibility. Windows retains a Path receiver and seven stack dwords for position, sweep, and a borrowed SubLoc; accepted entry seeds the Player-embedded FollowState and clears the Player-owned exit-pending gate.

Android retains the SearchPos name and evaluates the local probe subtraction in the same vector-expression order. The byte-identical Windows candidate confirms the two by-value vectors, borrowed TrackRowCell pointer, bool return, and Path ownership; the second vector remains ABI-owned but unused in this platform split.

entries.ios_symbol: cRPath::BuildHill(int, float, float, bool, char*, char*)
entries.notes: Windows helper name keeps the recovered hill/valley role while iOS exposes the BuildHill spelling.

entries.ios_symbol: cRPath::BuildSBend(int, float, float, bool, char*, char*)

entries.notes: Windows and Android validate caller-owned cRBodPos keyframes, retain an ObjectAnimation/frame graph on the destination Object, and generate vertices from current plus a scaled next-minus-current delta. Android's 0x74-byte keyframes and 0x18-byte animation record are port-specific; Windows proves 0x80-byte keyframes and a 0x14-byte ObjectAnimation.

Both initialize the global RShell state, DAT index, scratch and music buffers, registered-sound count, input slots, and key-repeat state. The platform ports use different scratch sizes but preserve the same ownership.
Both prefer the loaded DAT index unless the boolean formal forces a direct filesystem probe.
The ports share the caller-sized archive or filesystem read contract, including the DAT data-offset sentinel and caller-owned destination buffer.
The variable-size RShell loader reports the selected byte count, reads through the loaded DAT stream when possible, and otherwise owns the filesystem fallback allocation.
The exact Windows wrapper supplies a null destination to the three-argument RShellLoadFile overload.
The Windows implementation walks intermediate directories before deleting the final component; iOS delegates the same public RShell contract to MacDeleteFile.
The Windows implementation creates intermediate path context and writes the caller-owned byte range; iOS delegates the same public RShell contract to MacSaveFile.
The exact four-formal signature names the caller-owned 128-byte filename records. Windows implements DAT/filesystem enumeration locally; the iOS RShell entry is an empty platform stub.
Both decode the DAT header and full index, rebase each 12-byte entry path against the installed index owner, and retain an open backing stream.
This returns the global RShell scratch workspace, not DAT payload ownership; Windows allocates a 4 MiB workspace while iOS allocates 5 MiB.
This returns the second RShell scratch region rather than an end sentinel: Windows adds 0x200000 to its 4 MiB workspace, while iOS adds 0x280000 to its 5 MiB workspace.
Windows adds guarded sentinels and tracked-stack accounting around the RShell allocation; iOS retains the public size and label contract while delegating to malloc.
Windows validates and removes a guarded tracked allocation; iOS retains the public pointer contract while delegating to free.
Both snapshot the current RShell allocation depth for a later unwind; the saved-field offset differs between the platform-specific allocation-stack layouts.
The Android body has the same ASCII fold and right-terminator success contract as Windows, including the prefix-friendly case where the right argument ends first.
All ports scan to a sign, dot, or digit, advance the caller-owned cursor, accumulate decimal digits and scale, and return a float; the recovered Windows source is instruction-exact.
Both methods return byte zero of the player-owned mouse subobject. Windows stores that cRMouse-compatible prefix at GamePlayer +0x16c.
Both methods snapshot live x/y into +0x0c/+0x10 before enabling byte zero; the platform-specific cursor bridge differs after the shared prefix.
The iPhone ARM and Windows x86 bodies share the same active guard, saved-to-live x/y restore at +0x0c/+0x10 to +0x04/+0x08, and byte-zero clear.

Both populate the borrowed Object/cRObject slot. The iOS epilogue establishes no return value and every Windows caller discards EAX, proving the Windows cdecl helper is a staticized void load rather than a cursor-returning API.

The real Windows value-returning operator is exact at 49/49 instructions. Its stack output pointer and eight-byte callee cleanup are the VC6 hidden-sret ABI; the exact Bod consumer retains an explicit compatibility view because natural temporary spellings stop dereferencing the returned EAX pointer.

The exact 11/11 Windows static stdcall body takes two const-reference operands and returns the dot product in x87 ST0 as double. Android independently preserves the authored static overload; iOS exports only the adjacent member overload.

The exact 10/10 Windows member takes one const-reference operand. Exact and partial consumers spill or compare a four-byte result, proving the source return is float even though the x87 callee body alone cannot distinguish float from double.

Windows callee cleanup proves the complete matrix is passed by value, matching iOS. Native establishes no EAX result and both callers discard it, so the honest Windows source is a void operator*=. Consistent matrix-times-source product spelling closes the body to an exact 40/40 instructions while preserving both caller baselines.

The exact 38/38 Windows member accepts a const matrix reference and preserves its receiver in EAX. Two sprite-facing callsites immediately dereference that result, proving a receiver reference/pointer contract rather than void; all direct const-reference spellings preserve their caller baselines.
The exact 29/29 Windows member mutates its ECX receiver and returns the prior magnitude as float. This corrects the old analysis-only double fastcall declaration without changing generated code.
The exact 19/19 Windows member only reads its receiver and returns a float magnitude, establishing the same const ownership exported by both mobile binaries.

The exact 12/12 Windows member copies one const-reference source into its receiver and tail-calls the no-argument normalize member. Android preserves the authored overload; no corresponding iOS export is present.

The exact 40/40 Windows member takes two const-reference operands and copies a function-local static result into its ECX receiver. Native establishes no EAX result, proving the source return is void; the reference signature also reproduces the VC6 local-static mangling exactly.

Android retains both emitted single-float constructor symbols. A real Windows TransformMatrix(float) definition is exact at 21/21 instructions; its EAX receiver is the VC6 constructor ABI, while Android and iOS expose a distinct mutating tMatrix::Scale(float) member.

The exact 50/50 Windows member mixes the matrix's up and forward basis rows in place, which is rotation about its local X axis. Both mobile symbol tables preserve the authored RotLocalX name; all direct Windows callers remain byte-identical under the corrected alias.

The exact 50/50 Windows member mixes the matrix's right and forward basis rows in place, proving local-Y rotation. Both mobile symbol tables preserve the authored RotLocalY name; the old world-axis label was only an analysis guess.

The exact 50/50 Windows member mixes the matrix's right and up basis rows in place, proving local-Z rotation. Both mobile symbol tables preserve the authored RotLocalZ name, and the path/camera callsites retain identical machine code after adopting it.

entries.ios_symbol: tMatrix::tMatrix(float, float, float, float, float, float, float, float, float, float, float, float, float, float, float, float)
entries.android_symbol: tMatrix::tMatrix(float, float, float, float, float, float, float, float, float, float, float, float, float, float, float, float)
entries.notes: The real Windows 16-float constructor is exact at 34/34 instructions. Its EAX result is the ordinary VC6 constructor receiver ABI; large partial callers retain a documented initializer compatibility view only where direct temporary syntax changes stack and register allocation.

The exact 114/114 Windows member takes two const-reference operands and writes their affine product into its receiver. Its last live EAX value is merely the right operand; both wrappers discard it, and a void definition remains instruction-identical.

The exact 18/18 Windows member snapshots its receiver and delegates to the two-reference multiply core, matching the mobile one-argument Multiply method. It returns void: native only inherits the core's incidental rhs register, and every direct caller discards it.
The exact 19/19 Windows thiscall member resets all four matrix rows, including translation. Both mobile symbol tables preserve the authored no-argument Identity method.
The exact 16/16 Windows thiscall member resets the basis and homogeneous lanes while preserving translation. Both mobile symbol tables preserve the authored RotIdentity name.

The exact Windows void spelling remains 54/54 instructions, and its sole caller discards EAX. Both Android callers likewise ignore r0; the ARM body ends immediately after its final position store, leaving only the incidental receiver register rather than constructing a result.

The exact Windows void spelling remains 55/55 instructions. All ordinary Windows and Android callers discard the result register; the only tail calls are from independently void cRPlayer/cROverlay AI callbacks, and the ARM helper returns directly after its last matrix store without establishing a value.

The exact 26/26 Windows void thiscall member normalizes each basis row and rebuilds the right and forward axes through cross products. Both mobile symbol tables retain the authored Orthoganalize spelling.
The exact 57/57 Windows member takes one const-reference direction, normalizes it into the forward row, and rebuilds the other basis rows. The signature and behavior agree with both mobile symbols.
The exact 22/22 Windows member takes a const-reference target, forms target minus position, and delegates to SetZDir. Both mobile symbol tables preserve the same method and parameter ownership.

The exact 26/26 Windows body is a void Quaternion::operator=(const AxisAngle&) conversion: constructor syntax adds a required receiver return absent from native, while assignment syntax preserves the body and its sole Interpolate callsite exactly. Android moved the analogous conversion to a constructor.

The exact 30/30 Windows body and its sole Interpolate callsite match void AxisAngle::operator=(const Quaternion&). Android retains the same operator name; the Itanium symbol does not encode its return type, while Windows establishes no EAX result.

The typed Windows const-reference constructor remains an honest 92.47% match with 186/186 instruction parity, a 42-instruction prefix, and 23 clean operands. The remaining differences are fallback branch layout, not ownership or ABI uncertainty.
The real Windows const-reference constructor is exact at 82/82 instructions with three clean operands and no explicit source return.

The exact 105/105 Windows body uses an implicit Quaternion copy assignment between extraction and lane snapping, then converts through the owned AxisAngle assignments and matrix constructor. Its sole Windows caller discards EAX; both Android callers discard r0 and all three ARM exits establish no result, proving the authored member returns void.

The exact 61/61 Windows void member takes two const-reference matrices, interpolates relative rotation, re-orthogonalizes, and blends translation. Both mobile symbol tables preserve the complete authored signature.

Both bodies store the four float lanes at +0/+4/+8/+12. Windows additionally establishes eax as the receiver before the stores; the pointer-returning tColour method remains exact at 10/10 instructions and is consumed directly by many callsites.

The exact 3/3 Windows body stores the float argument at tColour +0x0c. Both Windows callers discard eax, and void float source produces the native mov/mov/ret sequence without a synthetic integer return.
Windows and Android store one intensity into r/g/b and force alpha to 1.0f. The exact 7/7 Windows helper is void; callers do not consume the incidental argument bits left in eax.

This is the stable Windows harness name for the four-float constructor role. Both implementations write RGBA at +0/+4/+8/+12; the exact 9/9 Windows source is a void write and all covered callers ignore the final component register.
The exact 7/7 Windows body and Android overload store r/g/b at +0/+4/+8 while preserving alpha. The sole Windows caller uses it as a void mutator.
Both ports implement White through the grayscale helper with 1.0f. The Windows wrapper is exact at 3/3 instructions with its call target resolved.
Both ports implement Black through the grayscale helper with 0.0f. The Windows wrapper is exact at 3/3 instructions with its call target resolved.
Windows and Android both walk 12-byte path/sample-id/normalization records until an empty path. Android additionally names the corresponding global gSFXBank and stores its address in gRSound.
iOS exposes the cRSprite owner family but not a direct constructor symbol.
iOS also exposes `cRSpriteManager::LoadSet(char*, int, int)`; Windows texture registration is the direct single-texture split.
The iOS name confirms the matrix-driven facing/tail family; Windows keeps the narrower facing-angle helper name.

All three implementations return the sprite texture record's +0x98 payload. The mobile GetTextureRef accessor is a distinct function returning record +0x8c; the Windows stable harness name is therefore historically misleading.
The exact Windows helper and mobile bodies fold only lowercase ASCII a through z by subtracting 32.
The exact Windows copy preserves the mobile NUL-terminated behavior and adds a Windows-only Dodgy Rstrcpy diagnostic for high-bit bytes.
The ARM and x86 bodies both treat the first argument as the pattern, scan the second argument, fold compared bytes through RstrASC, and return a cursor into the searched string.
The exact Windows and mobile helpers return the byte after the next CRLF pair only when a non-NUL byte follows it.
The exact Windows helper and mobile body scan to the next numeric token, consume an optional minus, accumulate decimal digits, and update the caller-owned cursor.

Both build the mode-selection front end with Tutorial, Postal Adventure or Postal Mode, Time Trial, Challenge Mode, Help, and Back after the same fade, menu music, Menubg backdrop, and centered border setup. The mobile port also exposes a Pro-Challenge button, so its widget tail is one pointer larger than the exact 0x48-byte Windows owner.

Both dispatch the same mode/help/back widgets and run the owner-local random replay-attract controller through the shared front-end and subgame handoff. Windows omits the mobile-only Pro-Challenge branch.
All ports initialize the global cRLoadingBar lifecycle owner. Windows preserves an exact 0x0c-byte state object at data_503290 and the broader renderer setup remains honestly partial.
The exact Windows member releases both loading textures and preserves the measured budget through the cRLoadingBar owner.
The exact Windows AI member advances and renders the global loading bar through the same owner exported as gLoadingBar on mobile.
Both initialize the cRLogo-owned row or image-object bank before any text script is opened. Windows keeps 32 fixed 0x90-byte image donors; iOS v1.5 initializes 100 port-specific cRLogoRows.

The symbol-bearing ARM body independently performs the same fade, IntroText music, SpaceRed backdrop, star unhide, script load, Text Start/Text End parse, glyph and image construction, Duration velocity assignment, and final RShellMemoryFree. The no-argument cRIntro::Init is a distinct border-heavy screen and was the previous incorrect mapping.
iOS v1.9 preserves the authored UnInit symbol; the v1.5 cRLogo::AI body inlines the same active-letter list teardown visible in the exact Windows helper.
Both handle skip input, fade completion, normalized crawl progress, and cRLogo-owned letter teardown. The Windows body factors UnInit into destroy_intro_screen.

The exact Windows 0x90-byte record and the port-specific cRLogoLetter both integrate velocity, fade alpha across the same z window, dirty the borrowed object, and toggle visibility. Mobile cRLogoRow::AI retains the same update body for its row records.
The exact Windows initializer operates on the 8-byte DuplicateVertices owner embedded at DirectXLoader +0x5e08 and allocates count times 10 bytes for its records.
The exact Windows member constructs the same cROptions lifecycle owner, whose complete 0x24-byte extent is embedded at GameRoot +0x4f388.
All ports tear down the cROptions widget set and persist the active configuration through the same lifecycle edge.
The exact Windows state machine operates on the root-owned cROptions fields and matches the cross-port lifecycle role.

Windows callers pass the cROptions receiver but no bool and the exact body only applies the two audio scalars. This is the no-argument audio subset of the broader mobile Apply lifecycle, not a signature-identical mapping.
The exact Windows lifecycle edge kills every owned sprite, clears the fixed 0x2c-byte entry records, and resets the root-owned cRStarManager state.
The exact Windows member allocates the cRStarManager entry array and seeds its requested count before conditionally initializing the visual effect.
All ports initialize the same root-owned cRStarManager and its 0x2c-byte streak entries. The near-exact Windows source retains four honest scheduling residuals.
The exact Windows four-state lifecycle callback is the cRStarManager AI method installed through the inherited BodBase callback slot.
All ports advance and respawn the same cRStarManager streak entries; the Windows member is near-exact with two honest scheduling swaps.
The exact Windows member initializes the single-widget cRHelp owner embedded at SubgameRuntime +0x1260008.
The exact Windows lifecycle edge tears down the shared border set through the four-byte cRHelp owner.
The exact Windows member handles the Back widget and returns the root front-end flow to the New Game screen.

All ports build the high-score UI through the void cRHighScore lifecycle. Windows embeds it at GameRoot +0x12e6e50; five ten-handle row banks prove storage through +0xf4, while the following 0x14 root bytes remain unassigned before TipManager. All three Windows callers discard EAX.

The exact void Windows teardown stores cRHighScore +0x08, kills the active border set, and clears the owning root player's pending-entry state. Android AI tail-branches here, and all six Windows callers discard EAX.

The ports share the void cRHighScore name-entry, bank-toggle, replay-launch, and exit state machine. Android directly tail-branches among Init, UnInit, and Exit; the Windows void transcription is proof-grade at 205/205 instructions.
The exact void Windows member restores the postal or challenge front-end state and requests the corresponding rebuild through the root-owned cRHighScore controller. Both Windows callers discard EAX.
All ports insert a postal or arcade result through the cRSubHighScore owner. Windows receives the working cRSubSolution explicitly before value-copying it into the owned top-ten array.
The Windows member copies the working cRSubSolution into the cRSubHighScore survival result and pending-entry storage before ordered insertion; mobile versions source the current solution internally.
All ports update the per-route time-trial solution through cRSubHighScore. Windows additionally receives the working cRSubSolution pointer explicitly.

entries.ios_symbol: cRSubSolution::ReSet(int, int, float, int, int, int)
entries.android_symbol: cRSubSolution::ReSet(int, int, float, int, int, int)
entries.notes: The exact Windows thiscall body resets the complete 0x1fac0-byte replay/high-score entry and its 21,600 six-byte run records. The third Windows argument is carried as the raw float bits expected by the cross-port signature.
The Windows thiscall receiver is the embedded SubHighScore/cRSubHighScore owner; the only caller is the Cancel branch of cRHighScore::AI().
The exact Windows method validates and expands the compact header plus its three replay lanes into the same 0x1fac0-byte cRSubSolution owner.
The exact Windows method is the inverse persistence bridge, writing the compact header and variable replay lanes from one cRSubSolution.

Windows callsites seed `this` with SubgameRuntime +0xff25e0, whose 0x330-byte extent is fixed by the following PathManager and the runtime size ledger. The exact body writes the shared gTimeTrialString buffer and does not read receiver fields.

The void Windows member initializes the same 0x28-byte GUI owner and mode-dependent widget slots. Android independently preserves the identical borrowed game pointer and +0x04 through +0x24 widget layout; the sole Windows caller discards EAX. Removing the synthetic result leaves 167/167 instructions at 96.41%.
Both implementations branch on modes 0, 1, and 4 and destroy the corresponding widgets from the same GUI fields. The Windows member is exact at 96/96 instructions.
The ports share the cRGUI owner and postal, challenge, and time-trial level-selection state machine; the broad Windows body remains honestly partial.

For the GUI callsite, the exact four-instruction Windows helper is cRGUI::Open: it stores the root-owned cRSubGame pointer at GUI +0x00. The linker folded the identical ThanksScreen owner-binding body into the same address, so the stable Windows harness name remains generic.

This is the Windows Direct3D variant of the same per-object render-colour operation: it packs the float tint into tColourSmall and rewrites grouped diffuse vertices instead of updating OpenGL state. Android proves the authored packed owner and void contract; all 26 Windows callsites discard EAX, and the void source remains exact at 47/47 instructions.

The bodies agree exactly on the class layout and conversion role: float RGBA is multiplied by 255 and stored at byte offsets +2, +1, +0, and +3. Android G0SetColour reads the same record as red, green, blue, and alpha, proving the shared four-byte BGRA owner; the Windows harness keeps its stable descriptive function name.
